focus_areas: Auth endpoints (OAuth2/OIDC/SAML, JWT lifecycle, identity linking)
REST API authorization (x-tmi-authz, PATCH, nested/batch, ACL)
Admin API & DB-resident settings (step-up, encryption, audit sink)
Worker pipeline (NATS envelopes, Object Store, result consumer)
Server-side egress & delegated content OAuth (SSRF, HMAC, tokens)

findings.title: No per-component NATS subject scoping; any worker can publish results for any job
findings.description: The result consumer subscribes to 'jobs.result.>' (api/result_consumer.go:175) and worker Publish (internal/worker/nats.go:127) has no authorization wrapper. No NATS-level accounts/creds restrict a worker to the subjects of jobs it actually consumed, and handleResult/MarkTerminal accept any JobID with no publisher-identity check. Any compromised component can publish jobs.result.{any_job_id}, flipping other jobs' terminal state and firing their webhooks. Concrete code anchor for the threat model's T19 gap.
findings.exploit_scenario: A compromised chunk-embed worker publishes Result{JobID: <another user's in-flight job>} to jobs.result.<id>; the monolith marks that job terminal and emits its webhook event, corrupting the extraction pipeline cross-user.
findings.recommendation: Per-component NATS accounts/credentials scoping publish/subscribe subjects (and Object Store buckets) per worker; alternatively per-job inbox subjects created by the controller. Treat as a design-level requirement, not deployment config.
findings.confidence_reason: Code-verified: wildcard subscription, no publisher identity checks anywhere in the path; concrete instance of known T19 gap (triage should merge with T19).

findings.title: Result envelope fields trusted without validation (unbounded ReasonDetail, unvalidated ReasonCode/JobID)
findings.description: The Result envelope is unmarshaled from untrusted NATS data (api/result_consumer.go:248) with no field validation. ReasonDetail is persisted into documents.access_reason_detail as NullableDBText with NO size cap (api/models.go:400, document_store_gorm.go:604-631); ReasonCode has size:64 only at schema level (truncation, not rejection); JobID is not bound to the publishing worker. NATS's default 1 MiB message cap is the only practical bound, and it is not enforced in code.
findings.exploit_scenario: Compromised worker publishes results with ~1 MiB ReasonDetail strings repeatedly, bloating the DB; or claims failure/success states for jobs it never processed, injecting attacker-chosen text into document diagnostics and webhook-visible state.
findings.recommendation: Validate the Result envelope before persistence: cap ReasonDetail (e.g. 4 KB), validate ReasonCode against a known-code enum and length, and reject results whose JobID was not issued to the publishing component (pairs with F-001's subject scoping).
findings.confidence_reason: All three unvalidated fields confirmed in code with no runtime checks; matches owner-confirmed 'no result-blob validation' gap with concrete fields.

findings.title: ResultRef not bound to JobID before Object Store deletion; cross-job blob deletion
findings.description: handleResult passes res.Output.ResultRef from the untrusted Result envelope to DeletePayload (api/result_consumer.go:118-119). payloadName (internal/worker/nats.go:117-123) checks only the 'TMI_PAYLOADS/' prefix and never verifies the name belongs to res.JobID. Blob names follow the pattern {jobID}/extracted and {jobID}/result, so a forged ResultRef can target any other in-flight job's blob.
findings.exploit_scenario: Worker publishes Result{JobID:'job-xyz', ResultRef:'TMI_PAYLOADS/job-123/extracted'}; the monolith deletes job-123's intermediate blob, breaking that job's processing (integrity/availability of other users' extractions).
findings.recommendation: Validate that the object name after 'TMI_PAYLOADS/' starts with the envelope's own JobID before any delete; combine with per-component bucket scoping (F-001).
findings.confidence_reason: Binding completely absent across the DeletePayload path; verified blob naming pattern makes cross-job targeting trivial for a compromised worker.

findings.title: OAuth BaseProvider uses raw http.Client without redirect blocking, bypassing SafeHTTPClient
findings.description: BaseProvider builds an http.Client with only Timeout + otel transport (auth/provider.go:130-133) — no CheckRedirect, no SafeHTTPClient SSRF validation or IP pinning. Used for token exchange (lines 167, 219, 339) and userinfo fetches (line 278). Default Go behavior follows up to 10 redirects, so a configured (or compromised) provider endpoint can redirect token/userinfo requests — carrying client_secret in POST bodies — to internal targets. Inconsistent with the #345 'all direct http.Client callers migrated to SafeHTTPClient with lint guard' control claimed in the threat model.
findings.exploit_scenario: Provider TokenURL (admin/operator-configured) points at or redirects to http://169.254.169.254/ or an internal service; client_secret is POSTed to the internal endpoint and/or internal response contents are parsed and logged.
findings.recommendation: Route BaseProvider requests through SafeHTTPClient, or at minimum set CheckRedirect to refuse redirects (http.ErrUseLastResponse) and apply the ssrf_validator to resolved targets. Extend the #345 lint guard to the auth package.
findings.confidence_reason: Raw client and redirect-following confirmed; requires operator/admin-level endpoint configuration but contradicts the project's own claimed control, and redirect-following reaches targets the admin never configured.

findings.title: SAML LogoutRequest signature not verified
findings.description: ProcessLogoutRequest (auth/saml/provider.go:259-286) parses the LogoutRequest with xml.Unmarshal (line 268) and checks only issuer match (line 273) and NameID presence (line 277) — no signature verification, unlike the login path's ParseXMLResponse. The handler comment in auth/handlers_saml.go (~line 320) claims '(includes signature verification)' which is false; the handler then invalidates user sessions based on the unverified NameID (lines 330-347). No IssueInstant/conditions validation either.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker submits a forged SAML LogoutRequest with a victim's email as NameID to the SLO endpoint; the server invalidates the victim's sessions — targeted, unauthenticated session revocation (forced logout DoS).
findings.recommendation: Verify the LogoutRequest's XML signature against the IdP certificate (crewjam/saml's validation) before any session invalidation; validate IssueInstant freshness. Fix the misleading handler comment.
findings.confidence_reason: Code confirms missing verification and a comment falsely claiming it exists; impact is targeted session revocation (availability), not account takeover, which tempers severity.

findings.title: SAML user matching by email only — missing the cross-provider conflict check the OAuth path has
findings.description: SAMLManager.processUser (auth/saml_manager.go:257-291) matches existing users via GetUserByEmail (line 260) without checking whether the matched user is bound to a different provider. The OAuth path explicitly rejects this (errCrossProviderConflict, auth/handlers_oauth_user.go:146-152) and uses tiered (provider, sub) matching; SAML skips that flow entirely. The #383 identity-link hardening covers OAuth, not SAML. A SAML assertion with a victim's email from any configured IdP yields a JWT for the victim's OAuth-created account.
findings.exploit_scenario: Victim signs in via Google OAuth. A SAML IdP is later configured that an attacker can influence (attacker-controlled sub-realm, compromised IdP, or misconfigured trust). Attacker asserts victim@example.com via SAML and is issued the victim's account JWT — full account takeover.
findings.recommendation: Apply the OAuth tiered matching to SAML: match (provider, ProviderUserID) first, then (provider, email); reject email-only matches where user.Provider differs from the asserting provider, mirroring errCrossProviderConflict.
findings.confidence_reason: Asymmetry code-verified (OAuth has the check, SAML does not); precondition of a trusted-but-attacker-influenced IdP is real in federated enterprise deployments.

findings.title: Secret-classified settings stored plaintext when encryption is unconfigured; no write-time enforcement, no automatic re-encryption
findings.description: SettingsService.Set encrypts only if encryptor is configured and enabled (api/settings_service.go:346); the Secret classification (classification_registry.go) gates API masking but not write-time encryption. The startup check (cmd/server/startup_checks.go, warnIfPlaintextSecretsAtRest) is log-only — ERROR in production but never aborts startup. ReEncryptAll exists only as a manual admin endpoint (POST /admin/settings/reencrypt, config_handlers.go:773) and fails unless encryption was enabled first — plaintext secrets written before enablement persist until an operator notices.
findings.exploit_scenario: Deployment starts without SettingsEncryptionKey; admin writes auth.jwt.secret / timmy.llm_api_key via the settings API; values sit plaintext in system_settings. A later DB compromise or backup exfiltration recovers them; enabling encryption later does not remediate existing rows.
findings.recommendation: Enforce at write time: reject (or force-encrypt) any Secret-classified setting when encryption is disabled; refuse production startup with plaintext secret-classified rows present; auto-trigger re-encryption when the key is first configured.
findings.confidence_reason: Verified: warning is non-fatal in production, ReEncryptAll is manual and pre-enable-guarded; a hard invariant (secret ⇒ encrypted) is unenforced with no automatic remediation path.

findings.title: Unbounded io.ReadAll on OAuth provider responses (token exchange and userinfo error paths)
findings.description: customTokenExchange calls io.ReadAll(resp.Body) with no LimitReader (auth/provider.go:227); fetchEndpoint repeats the pattern (line 327). No Transport-level response cap exists. A single response from a hostile or compromised provider endpoint is read fully into monolith memory.
findings.exploit_scenario: Provider endpoint (configured to attacker infrastructure, or a compromised legitimate provider) answers a token exchange with an HTTP 400 and a multi-GB body; the monolith allocates it all — memory exhaustion from one request.
findings.recommendation: Wrap with io.LimitReader (e.g. 1 MiB) before ReadAll at both sites; audit other response readers in the auth package for the same pattern.
findings.confidence_reason: Both unbounded reads confirmed with no mitigations; unbounded single-response allocation driven by an attacker-controllable endpoint is the reportable subspecies of resource exhaustion.

findings.title: Unbounded recursion over HTML parse tree — duplicate copy runs in the MONOLITH process
findings.description: extractTextFromHTML walks the parse tree with a depth-unbounded recursive closure (pkg/extract/html.go:46-60); an identical copy exists at api/timmy_content_provider_http.go:82-96. golang.org/x/net/html.Parse builds full-depth trees with no cap, and the 10 MiB fetch limit still permits ~1M nested elements — far past Go stack limits. Stack overflow in Go is an unrecoverable fatal error (not recoverable via recover()). The pkg/extract copy runs in the sandboxed worker (contained), but the timmy_content_provider_http.go copy runs in the monolith API process.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker registers an HTTP content source pointing at a page of deeply nested divs; the monolith's HTTPEmbeddingSource parses it and the recursive walk overflows the stack, crashing the entire API server.
findings.recommendation: Convert both copies to an iterative walk with an explicit stack, or add a depth counter (e.g. 1000) that aborts extraction; deduplicate the two implementations so the fix lands once.
findings.confidence_reason: Both copies confirmed depth-unbounded; the monolith-resident copy upgrades this from contained worker DoS to API-server crash; only mitigation is practical stack depth, not a code guard.

findings.title: VisibilityInternal settings filtered from GET but not from LIST; PUT accepts VisibilityInternal keys
findings.description: GetSystemSetting rejects VisibilityInternal (api/config_handlers.go:503), but ListSystemSettings → mergeSettingsWithConfig applies filterByVisibility only to config-derived settings (line 390); database rows are merged unfiltered (lines 396-430). Compounding it, UpdateSystemSetting (line 561) validates reserved and config-controlled keys but never checks ClassificationFor(key).Visibility — so an admin can WRITE a VisibilityInternal key into the DB via PUT and then read it via LIST, an asymmetry the GET/DELETE paths were built to prevent.
findings.exploit_scenario: Admin (or any path that writes settings) PUTs a VisibilityInternal key; LIST /admin/settings then exposes it with its value despite individual GET returning 404 — bypassing the visibility classification for internal/bootstrap settings.
findings.recommendation: Apply filterByVisibility to dbSettings in the merge; reject VisibilityInternal keys in UpdateSystemSetting; add a test that LIST never returns VisibilityInternal regardless of DB contents.
findings.confidence_reason: Asymmetric filtering and the missing PUT-side classification check both code-verified; actor is an authenticated admin so impact is visibility-policy bypass rather than privilege escalation.

findings.title: XML depth limit bypassed inside DecodeElement-consumed subtrees
findings.description: boundedXMLDecoder enforces MaxXMLElementDepth in Token() (pkg/extract/ooxml_common.go:250-267), but DecodeElement (lines 275-281) consumes subtrees internally without routing through Token() — the code's own comment (lines 233-239) acknowledges depth inside those subtrees is unbounded. DecodeElement is used on text elements in DOCX footnotes and PPTX notes. Go 1.20+ encoding/xml's internal ~10k depth limit is the only backstop, and it's an undocumented implementation detail.
findings.exploit_scenario: Adversarial OOXML nests deeply inside a DecodeElement-consumed element; the wrapper's depth limit never sees it. Bounded today by Go's internal limit to a worker panic/DLQ churn rather than unbounded stack growth.
findings.recommendation: Replace DecodeElement on untrusted subtrees with a Token() loop under the bounded decoder, or track depth explicitly across DecodeElement calls.
findings.confidence_reason: Real, self-documented bypass; blast radius contained by Go runtime internals and worker isolation, hence LOW despite high certainty.

findings.title: Delegated-token AES-GCM encryption lacks AAD binding to (user, provider) — encrypted-blob swapping
findings.description: ContentTokenEncryptor Seal/Open pass nil AAD (api/content_token_encryption.go:47,58); ciphertexts have no cryptographic binding to their row. An attacker with a write-capable DB primitive (e.g. an UPDATE-only SQL injection that can copy but not decrypt) can graft user A's encrypted delegated token into user B's row; decryption succeeds and B's flows use A's Google Drive/MS365/Confluence token.
findings.exploit_scenario: A constrained DB-write primitive copies the victim's encrypted access_token blob into the attacker's user_content_token row; the attacker's content-source flows now operate with the victim's delegated token against their external systems.
findings.recommendation: Pass AAD binding the ciphertext to its context — e.g. userID + ':' + providerID — in both Seal and Open; rotate/re-encrypt existing rows on upgrade.
findings.confidence_reason: nil-AAD confirmed; meaningful only against write-limited DB primitives since full DB compromise already allows worse — a real but defense-in-depth crypto gap.

findings.title: Mid-session role revocation does not stop broadcast delivery to revoked users
findings.description: Broadcast delivery (api/websocket.go:1540-1559) never re-checks authorization after connection establishment (comment at 2567-2568 states this explicitly); the WritePump heartbeat (~3544) re-checks only JWT expiry, not blacklist or role state. Mutations ARE re-checked per-operation (checkMutationPermission:2618). A user whose access is revoked mid-session keeps receiving diagram_operation_event broadcasts — including potentially confidential content — until disconnect/inactivity timeout (300s default). Concrete instance of threat-model T17.
findings.exploit_scenario: Org removes a user from a confidential threat model while they have a live collaboration session; they keep watching all edits in real time for up to the inactivity window.
findings.recommendation: Re-validate threat-model access (and JWT blacklist) on the heartbeat tick and close with a 4403 on failure — same fix tracked for T17 (#20-class work).
findings.confidence_reason: Gap confirmed in code; bounded by 300s inactivity timeout and already owner-acknowledged in the threat model — real but known and bounded.

findings.title: Step-up route table is fail-open; coverage not enforced against route registration
findings.description: StepUpRouteTable.Required returns false for any route not in the table (api/step_up_routes.go:49-54) and the middleware then passes through (step_up_middleware.go:35) — fail-open. Today all 31 /admin/* write operations in the OpenAPI spec are covered by the default policy, and no non-standard verbs exist; but nothing (test or CI) enforces that the spec and the Gin router stay synchronized, so future drift silently bypasses step-up.
findings.exploit_scenario: A future /admin route added to the router without a matching spec entry (or with an uncovered verb) skips the auth_time freshness check; a stale hijacked admin session can then mutate admin state.
findings.recommendation: Flip the default to fail-closed for any /admin/* write not explicitly in the table, and add a test asserting every registered admin write route has a step-up table entry.
findings.confidence_reason: Fail-open confirmed but zero currently-uncovered routes — purely future-drift hardening today.

findings.title: WebSocket ticket session_id cross-check skipped when ?session_id= omitted
findings.description: TicketValidator.ValidateTicket compares the ticket-bound sessionID to the query param only when the param is non-empty (cmd/server/jwt_auth.go:369-372), so omitting ?session_id= skips the cross-check the ws-ticket-auth design requires. Mitigations verified: tickets are 32-byte crypto-random, single-use (consumed on first validation), 30s TTL, and post-upgrade ValidateSessionAccess still authorizes the ticket's user against the threat model/diagram in the URL path — so cross-resource access is still blocked; the gap is session-integrity, not authz.
findings.exploit_scenario: A leaked/captured ticket for session A is replayed (within 30s, before first use) against a different session URL without ?session_id=; the connection binds the ticket owner's identity to a session the ticket wasn't issued for — but only on resources that user could already access.
findings.recommendation: Make the cross-check mandatory: reject when ?session_id= is absent or mismatched, per the design doc.
findings.confidence_reason: Code gap is real and contradicts the design doc, but entropy, single-use, short TTL, and post-upgrade authz reduce residual impact to session-integrity.

findings.title: fixOwnerField fabricates User objects from string /owner PATCH values after allowlist validation
findings.description: fixOwnerField (api/patch_utils.go:377-410) converts string values for /owner into User objects with attacker-supplied provider_id/email AFTER ValidatePatchAllowlist and patch application. Scoring verification found: the actor must already be the resource OWNER (the allowlist gates /owner to OwnerOnly), the fabricated owner only exists in memory, FK constraints block nonexistent users at store.Update, and audit/events emit only after a successful store — no privilege escalation path was identified.
findings.exploit_scenario: Owner PATCHes /owner with a bogus string; an in-memory User object with attacker-chosen fields exists transiently but persistence and event emission fail safely.
findings.recommendation: Reject string-typed /owner values at validation time instead of normalizing them post-application; keeps the type contract explicit and removes the transient fabricated object.
findings.confidence_reason: Verification downgraded: actor already holds max privilege on the resource and all persistence/audit paths fail closed — robustness bug, not a vulnerability.

findings.title: OIDC discovery fetch uses raw http.Client following redirects (startup-only, operator-configured)
findings.description: DiscoveryClient builds http.Client{Timeout} with no CheckRedirect or SafeHTTPClient validation (auth/oidc_discovery.go:60); discovery documents are fetched from configured issuer URLs and redirects are followed. Scoring verification found the issuer URLs come only from config file/env at process startup (validateOAuthProvidersAtStartup), not from runtime-mutable DB settings — making this an operator-controlled vector. The discovery doc's issuer field is validated after fetch, but the redirect target is contacted before that check.
findings.exploit_scenario: Operator-configured issuer redirects the well-known fetch to an internal endpoint at startup; response is parsed/logged. Requires control of startup config, which already implies host-level access.
findings.recommendation: Use SafeHTTPClient or set CheckRedirect to refuse redirects — cheap consistency with the #345 invariant even though the current vector is operator-only.
findings.confidence_reason: Raw client confirmed, but issuer URLs are startup-config-only (env/CLI operator vector — the DO-NOT-REPORT class); fix is desirable for invariant consistency, exploit path is weak.

findings.title: Owner-change detection uses struct equality instead of canonical identity comparison
findings.description: PatchThreatModel detects owner changes via Go struct equality (api/threat_model_handlers.go:688) over all fields including enrichment-populated DisplayName/Email. Scoring verification established the fragility only produces FALSE POSITIVES (spurious 'owner changing' → stricter gate applied unnecessarily): a true owner change always differs in provider_id, so it can never compare equal — the claimed gate-skip is logically impossible.
findings.exploit_scenario: None exploitable: enrichment differences over-trigger the owner gate (annoyance), never under-trigger it.
findings.recommendation: Compare canonical identity (provider, provider_id) via a helper for clarity and to avoid spurious gate triggers — code quality, not security.
findings.confidence_reason: Bypass direction is logically impossible; only defensive over-triggering occurs.

findings.title: Status-update HMAC signs body only, not the path delivery_id (replay within subscription)
findings.description: UpdateWebhookDeliveryStatus verifies the HMAC over bodyBytes only (api/webhook_delivery_handlers.go:256); delivery_id comes from the path and isn't in the signed payload. Cross-subscription replay is blocked (secret is looked up via the target delivery's SubscriptionID), so a signed (body, signature) pair can only be replayed against other deliveries in the SAME subscription — and the secret-holder could mint arbitrary signatures anyway, so replay adds capability only for a non-secret-holder who captured one signed sample.
findings.exploit_scenario: Eavesdropper who captured one signed status update (but not the secret) replays it against other delivery IDs in the same subscription, corrupting their status fields.
findings.recommendation: Include the delivery ID in the signed payload (deliveryID + body) to bind signatures to their target.
findings.confidence_reason: Technically valid binding gap, but marginal: secret-holders gain nothing, and the capture-without-secret scenario requires TLS-defeating eavesdropping.

findings.title: Participants broadcast hardcodes permissions:'writer' for every client
findings.description: broadcastParticipantsUpdate sets permissions := "writer" for all participants (api/websocket.go:2576) regardless of actual role; the REST path (convertClientToParticipant → getSessionPermissionsForUser) resolves real permissions correctly. Server-side mutation authz is intact (checkMutationPermission:2618 re-checks per operation). No client consumer makes security decisions on the broadcast field — UI-truth bug only.
findings.exploit_scenario: Collaborators see readers (or revoked users) listed as writers and may overshare based on the misleading panel — social-engineering value only.
findings.recommendation: Resolve actual roles in the broadcast path (reuse getSessionPermissionsForUser) so the wire metadata matches enforcement.
findings.confidence_reason: Server-side enforcement verified intact; purely informational metadata incorrectness.

findings.title: DOCX hyperlink targets concatenated into markdown unescaped (no rendering consumer found)
findings.description: handleHyperlinkEnd emits '[' + inner + '](' + target + ')' with no escaping or scheme validation (pkg/extract/docx.go:862); targets come from untrusted .rels XML. Scoring verification traced consumers: extracted markdown feeds the embedding/RAG pipeline (fenced as untrusted LLM input) and is NOT returned by document endpoints or rendered to HTML anywhere server-side; Note persistence sanitizes at the handler layer. The XSS chain requires a markdown→HTML renderer that does not currently exist.
findings.exploit_scenario: Only materializes if a future consumer renders extracted markdown to HTML without URL filtering; today the javascript: link is inert LLM context.
findings.recommendation: Escape ]()[ in targets and validate URL schemes (http/https/mailto) at extraction time — cheap immunization against future consumers.
findings.confidence_reason: Injection syntax real; no rendering consumer exists, so no current exploit chain — prompt-injection angle is already modeled as T6.

findings.title: Reserved SourceURL/SourceSecretRef envelope fields are schema-valid but unenforced (latent)
findings.description: Input carries RESERVED source_url/source_secret_ref/fetch_limits fields (pkg/jobenvelope/envelope.go:68-79); validation deliberately does not reject source-locator mode (validation.go:8,17). Grep confirms NO code path consumes these fields today. The 'extractor never holds a fetch credential' invariant is enforced by non-use only — already recorded as a tripwire in the threat model's open questions.
findings.exploit_scenario: Future source-locator implementation that skips egress-policy validation would let a publisher direct worker fetches at metadata endpoints — zero current exposure.
findings.recommendation: When implementing: validate SourceURL against the component's declared egress class (EgressNone must reject source-locator), and gate SourceSecretRef behind explicit per-component opt-in with audit logging.
findings.confidence_reason: Dormant by verified non-use; design tripwire, not a current vulnerability.

findings.title: Provider-secret masking uses a hardcoded suffix list instead of the classification registry
findings.description: isProviderSecretKey masks only keys ending in four hardcoded suffixes (api/config_handlers.go:33-38,54-72). Verification confirmed all CURRENT provider secrets are covered and the audit redactor uses broader contains-patterns as fallback; the risk is future provider fields with unlisted suffixes leaking plaintext in GET /admin/settings/{key} responses to authenticated admins.
findings.exploit_scenario: A future '.webhook_signing_secret'-style provider field returns unmasked to admin API consumers (and any response logging).
findings.recommendation: Drive masking from the classification registry's Secret flag (single source of truth) instead of a parallel suffix list.
findings.confidence_reason: No current key escapes the list; admin-only exposure; maintenance-drift concern.

findings.title: XLSX cell-count, decompression, and markdown-size limits are decoupled
findings.description: Three independent caps (compressed-size pre-check, excelize UnzipSizeLimit/UnzipXMLSizeLimit at pkg/extract/xlsx.go:86-88, markdown builder cap at ooxml_common.go:41-49) jointly bound XLSX processing, but no single invariant ties cell count to memory. Verification found excelize's 20MB per-part limit gates shared-strings materialization and the markdown cap trips before unbounded output — current defenses adequate.
findings.exploit_scenario: Only a concern if an operator raises/disables the markdown cap; no current path to unbounded allocation found.
findings.recommendation: Document the interaction of the three caps; consider a per-cell size bound for clarity.
findings.confidence_reason: Self-described as adequate-defenses/documentation; no concrete gap demonstrated.

findings.title: WebSocket Origin check uses prefix matching (blocked in practice by SameSite=Lax cookies)
findings.description: CheckOrigin validates via strings.HasPrefix(origin, allowed) (api/websocket.go:282), so 'https://example.com' admits 'https://example.com.attacker.com'. Scoring verification killed the exploit chain: WS authentication comes from the JWT extracted in the HTTP middleware before upgrade, and the access-token cookie is SameSite=Lax (auth/cookies.go:54) — a cross-site page cannot send it, so a hostile origin passes CheckOrigin but arrives unauthenticated.
findings.exploit_scenario: Would require SameSite policy regression or a token-bearing query/header a hostile page can set — neither exists today.
findings.recommendation: Replace HasPrefix with exact origin comparison (parse scheme+host+port) regardless — the check as written is one cookie-policy change away from being load-bearing.
findings.confidence_reason: Sloppy match confirmed but SameSite=Lax breaks the CSWSH chain; defense-in-depth fix recommended.

findings.title: Delegated token sent to Confluence apiBase (apiBase is hardcoded — non-finding)
findings.description: firstAccessibleResourceURL sends the user's delegated token to p.apiBase + '/oauth/token/accessible-resources'. Verification: apiBase is a private field set only from the hardcoded constant confluenceAPIBase = 'https://api.atlassian.com' (content_source_confluence.go:25); no setter, no config path. Only tests override it via package-internal assignment.
findings.exploit_scenario: None — the endpoint cannot be redirected at runtime; sending the token to the provider's API is inherent to the integration.
findings.recommendation: None required; keep apiBase hardcoded.
findings.confidence_reason: Premise (operator-configurable apiBase) is false; hardcoded constant verified.

findings.title: Document-lookup/terminal-flip ordering under at-least-once redelivery (handled safely)
findings.description: handleResult looks up the document (line 85) before MarkTerminal (line 103). Verification: MarkTerminal's guarded UPDATE only transitions non-terminal rows and gates webhook emission (transitioned && ok), the unknownDocumentRef sentinel intentionally handles deleted-document races, and the access-status update is idempotent. No duplicate emission or corruption path found.
findings.exploit_scenario: Redelivery after document deletion produces a sentinel row — operational noise, no attacker leverage.
findings.recommendation: None required; optionally log sentinel-row creation for observability.
findings.confidence_reason: Guarded UPDATE + sentinel handling verified safe; no exploitable window.

findings.title: Delivery-status GET HMAC signs only the delivery ID (intended dual-auth design)
findings.description: verifyDeliveryHMAC verifies HMAC over the delivery ID string (api/webhook_delivery_handlers.go:383). Verification: the secret is resolved via the delivery's own SubscriptionID, so a secret-holder can only read their own subscription's deliveries — which is exactly the authorized party in the dual-auth design; GET and POST sign different payloads so no cross-method replay exists.
findings.exploit_scenario: None beyond intended access: subscription secret-holder reads that subscription's delivery status.
findings.recommendation: Optionally include method+path in the MAC for future-proofing; no current exposure.
findings.confidence_reason: Design-as-intended; signature scope is sufficient for the authz it implements.

findings.title: PKCE get-then-delete race in state store (neutralized by code binding and verifier secrecy)
findings.description: GetPKCEChallenge (read) and DeletePKCEChallenge (line 445) are separate operations, leaving a concurrent-retrieval window. Verification: the state store is in-memory mutex-guarded (not shared Redis as the finding assumed), the challenge is bound to a single-use authorization code, and exchange still requires the secret code_verifier — no attacker-beneficial outcome survives.
findings.exploit_scenario: Racing requests duplicate a (public) challenge but cannot exchange without the verifier.
findings.recommendation: Use an atomic get-and-delete for hygiene (GETDEL-style) — not security-load-bearing today.
findings.confidence_reason: Race exists but multi-instance premise was wrong and PKCE verifier secrecy neutralizes it.

findings.title: DOCX image alt text concatenated into markdown unescaped (no rendering consumer found)
findings.description: handleDrawingDocPr emits '![' + descr + '](image-N)' unescaped (pkg/extract/docx.go:765). Same consumer analysis as the hyperlink finding: extracted markdown is consumed only by the fenced LLM/RAG pipeline; document endpoints never return extracted content; no server-side markdown→HTML rendering exists. XSS chain infeasible today; residual value is prompt-injection amplification, already modeled as T6.
findings.exploit_scenario: Inert unless a future consumer renders extracted markdown to HTML without URL filtering.
findings.recommendation: Escape markdown metacharacters in alt text at extraction time (same helper as hyperlink targets).
findings.confidence_reason: Injection syntax real, exploit chain absent — no renderer, content never reaches clients.

findings.title: PKCE material in content-OAuth error paths (challenge is public by design; verifier never exposed)
findings.description: Verification of Authorize (api/content_oauth_handlers.go:99-155): errors are logged server-side and clients get generic 500s; the code_challenge is sent to the authorization endpoint by PKCE design (not secret); the code_verifier is stored in the state payload and sent only to the token endpoint. No leak of secret material found.
findings.exploit_scenario: None — challenge exposure is harmless per RFC 7636; verifier never appears in responses or the auth URL.
findings.recommendation: Keep PKCE values out of log statements as hygiene; no code change required.
findings.confidence_reason: Finding mischaracterizes PKCE trust model; no secret material exposed.

findings.title: userAuthTime context extraction ignores exists flag (current guards are correct)
findings.description: Verification: the middleware's `!ok || authTime == nil` guard (api/step_up_middleware.go:42) safely handles absent keys, typed nils, and short-circuits before dereferencing; all auth paths set the key. The hypothesized failure requires simultaneously breaking the invariant AND removing the existing guards.
findings.exploit_scenario: None current; double-refactor hypothetical.
findings.recommendation: Optionally check the exists flag explicitly and document the middleware-ordering invariant.
findings.confidence_reason: Current code verified safe; purely hypothetical future-refactor concern.

findings.title: Dual-auth (HMAC-or-JWT) on delivery-status endpoint (intentional, correctly implemented)
findings.description: Verification: explicit if/else on hmacSignature presence — no weaker-leg confusion (empty signature routes to full JWT validation, which requires authenticated context). Exploitation requires a stolen admin JWT, which moots the endpoint-level concern.
findings.exploit_scenario: None beyond consequences of an already-compromised admin JWT.
findings.recommendation: None; design documented in code comments.
findings.confidence_reason: Intentional defense-in-depth with correct verification on both legs.

findings.title: No per-operation cell-count cap within the 64KB WS frame limit (volumetric, authorized actor)
findings.description: SetReadLimit(65536) bounds frames; processAndValidateCellOperations has no MaxCellsPerOperation. Verification: cost is linear in message size (no algorithmic amplification), the actor is an authenticated writer who can equivalently mutate via REST, and volumetric DoS by authorized users is explicitly deprioritized in the threat model.
findings.exploit_scenario: Authorized writer packs ~1000 cell ops per frame — linear resource use within their privileges.
findings.recommendation: Optional MaxCellsPerOperation (e.g. 100) as hygiene.
findings.confidence_reason: Linear cost, authorized actor, deprioritized category.

findings.title: Raw-HTML fallback on html.Parse error (error path practically unreachable)
findings.description: extractTextFromHTML returns raw input if html.Parse errors (pkg/extract/html.go:39-42). Verification: with strings.NewReader, html.Parse essentially never errors (no I/O failures; parser is browser-permissive), and the extracted text feeds embedding pipelines, not HTML renderers.
findings.exploit_scenario: Practically unreachable; even if reached, content lands in non-HTML consumers.
findings.recommendation: Return an error instead of raw content for hygiene.
findings.confidence_reason: Fallback unreachable in practice with in-memory readers; no exploitable surface.
